Literature_Source_Name(s): An AntibodyDrug Conjugate That Targets Tissue Factor Exhibits Potent Therapeutic Activity against a Broad Range of Solid Tumors
Literature_Source_Comments: TF-011 was chosen among the Abs tested. It is a highly potent human IgG1? monoclonal antibody engineered to target Tissue Factor (TF), which is aberrantly expressed across a wide range of solid tumors. Exhibiting high binding affinity and the ability to effectively compete with Factor VIIa (FVIIa), TF-011 robustly inhibits TF:FVIIa-mediated intracellular signaling pathways that drive tumor progression and metastasis. It mirrors the mechanism of action of trastuzumab by efficiently mediating Antibody-Dependent Cellular Cytotoxicity (ADCC) and demonstrating rapid internalization upon binding to TF, making it an ideal candidate for Antibody-Drug Conjugate (ADC) development. When conjugated with the cytotoxic agent Monomethyl Auristatin E (MMAE), forming TF-011-MMAE, it retains its targeting and effector functions while delivering a potent payload that induces selective tumor cell death both in vitro and in diverse patient-derived xenograft (PDX) models with heterogeneous TF expression. Importantly, TF-011-MMAE exhibits a favorable safety profile with minimal interference in coagulation processes, underscoring its potential as a therapeutic ADC.

Therapeutic potential of SGN-CD19B, a PBD-based anti-CD19 drug conjugate, for treatment of B-cell malignancies
Literature_Source_Comments: In Vitro Cytotoxicity of SGN-CD19B: Demonstrates broad activity against CD19+ tumor cell lines with notable sensitivity at or below 1 ng/mL. Cell lines with MYC, BCL2, and BCL6 rearrangements showed higher sensitivity, indicating a potential predictive biomarker for response. No strong correlation between CD19 expression levels and IC50 values, suggesting other factors contribute to drug sensitivity. Mechanism of Action of SGN-CD19B: Binds to CD19 on lymphoma cells, initiating internalization and endocytic trafficking, evident through colocalization with LAMP-1 in lysosomes. Induces DNA damage (evidenced by ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂ³-H2AX levels), apoptosis (activation of caspase 3/7 and cleaved PARP), and cell cycle arrest (G2/M phase), indicating a robust and multifaceted cytotoxic mechanism. In Vivo Activity of SGN-CD19B: Demonstrates significant tumor growth delay and durable complete regressions in B-NHL models at doses as low as 100 ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂ¼g/kg. Enhanced antitumor activity when combined with rituximab, particularly in follicular lymphoma models. In B-ALL models, significantly improves survival rates at various dosages without noticeable toxicity. B-Cell Depletion in Nonhuman Primates (NHPs): Dose-dependent depletion of circulating CD20+ B cells, with complete depletion at 100 ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂ¼g/kg. Histological analysis shows congruent B-lymphoid depletion in the spleen and reversible effects, suggesting a well-tolerated dose range. Comparative Analysis with Other ADCs: SGN-CD19B distinguishes itself from other ADCs by its potency, mechanism of action, linker stability, and uniform drug load. Demonstrates potent activity across a variety of malignant B-cell lines, including those with poor prognostic features, suggesting broad therapeutic potential. Less myelosuppression expected compared to other ADCs due to CD19's restricted expression to the B-cell lineage. Potential Clinical Implications and Future Directions: Promising preclinical results warrant further evaluation in clinical trials. Potential for activity across different subtypes of aggressive lymphomas and B-ALL, possibly including conditions with lower CD19 expression or poor prognostic indicators. Synergistic potential when combined with other therapies like rituximab, offering a strategic approach for treatment enhancement. Phase 1 dose escalation study is underway to assess therapeutic potential in relapsed/refractory B-NHL.

Targeting Multiple EGFR-expressing Tumors with a Highly Potent Tumor-selective AntibodyÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂDrug Conjugate
Literature_Source_Comments: Binding Properties and Affinity: ABBV-321 maintains comparable binding properties to its precursor molecules (AM1 and AM1 S238C) across various forms of EGFR, indicating that the S238C mutation and conjugation to the PBD dimer did not adversely affect binding affinity. FACS analysis confirmed that ABBV-321, AM1, and AM1 S238C bind to cells expressing wild-type and mutant EGFR forms with similar apparent affinity, reinforcing the specificity and potential efficacy of ABBV-321. In Vitro Cytotoxicity in GBM Tumor Cell Lines: ABBV-321 exhibits improved potency across a broad spectrum of GBM cell lines with varying levels of EGFR expression, suggesting its potential for broader application in GBM treatment compared to its predecessors. Notably, ABBV-321 is significantly effective even in non-EGFR-amplified GBM cell lines, indicating its potential to expand treatment options beyond tumors with high EGFR overexpression. In Vivo Efficacy in Tumor Models: ABBV-321 demonstrates substantial tumor growth inhibition and regression in various GBM tumor models with different EGFR genotypes, at lower doses compared to ABBV-221. The compound shows promise in combination therapy; lower doses of ABBV-321 combined with depatux-m effectively inhibit tumor growth, suggesting a synergistic effect and potential for reduced toxicity. ABBV-321 exhibits significant efficacy in various tumor models (lung, colorectal, head and neck, and mesothelioma), even those with low or moderate EGFR expression, broadening its therapeutic scope. In Vitro Cytotoxicity against Colorectal Tumor Cell Lines: ABBV-321 is cytotoxic to a wide range of colorectal cancer cell lines, including those with low to moderate EGFR expression levels, indicating a broader range of activity compared to auristatin-based ADCs like ABBV-221. The compound's potency in colorectal cancer cell lines suggests potential applicability in treating colorectal cancers, which historically have not responded well to microtubule inhibitors. Pharmacokinetics and Clinical Considerations: The pharmacokinetic profile of ABBV-321 in cynomolgus monkeys is favorable, with dose-proportional increases in concentration and a half-life that supports its therapeutic use. ABBV-321's high tumor selectivity and potent cytotoxicity position it as a promising clinical candidate for treating EGFR-positive tumors, potentially with a wider therapeutic window and reduced on-target toxicities compared to other PBD dimer-containing ADCs. Strategic Implications for Cancer Treatment: ABBV-321's efficacy across a range of tumor types, including those with lower levels of EGFR expression, suggests potential as a versatile, broad-spectrum anticancer agent. The compound's ability to retain activity in tumor models that are less responsive to traditional treatments like auristatin-based ADCs (e.g., in colorectal cancers) hints at a paradigm shift in treatment strategies for such tumors. The observation of enhanced potency and reduced toxicity in combination therapy points towards a strategic approach in cancer treatment, possibly allowing for lower dosing and reduced side effects while maintaining therapeutic efficacy.

PF-06804103, A Site-specific Anti-HER2 AntibodyÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂDrug Conjugate for the Treatment of HER2-expressing Breast, Gastric, and Lung Cancers
Literature_Source_Comments: The study investigated the in vivo efficacy of PF-06804103 in both T-DM1 eligible and ineligible breast cancer tumor models, including HER2-amplified tumors with high and low HER2 expression levels. In HER2-high expression models (like PDX-BRX-24312 and BT474-M1), PF-06804103 showed better efficacy than PT-DM1, leading to durable complete regressions that lasted more than two weeks after the last ADC dose. This trend continued in models with low HER2 expression, such as PDX-BRX-11380 (from a triple-negative breast cancer patient) and JIMT-1 (from a trastuzumab-refractory patient), where PF-06804103 was more potent than PT-DM1. Additionally, PF-06804103 demonstrated superior in vivo antitumor efficacy in HER2-expressing breast, gastric, and lung cancer xenograft models, outperforming PT-DM1 in multiple PDX (Patient-Derived Xenograft) and CLX (Cell Line Xenograft) tumors. Notably, in an in vivo model of PT-DM1 resistance developed using N87 tumors, PF-06804103 maintained its potency even after PT-DM1 resistance emerged, leading to sustained tumor regression after dosing cessation. This suggests that PF-06804103 can effectively overcome PT-DM1 resistance, making it a promising therapeutic option for patients with T-DM1-relapsed or refractory cancers.

ABBV-399, a c-Met AntibodyÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂDrug Conjugate that Targets Both METÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂAmplified and c-MetÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂOverexpressing Tumors, Irrespective of MET Pathway Dependence
Literature_Source_Comments: Conjugation and Binding Properties: ABBV-399 is a conjugate of ABT-700 and vcMMAE (a cytotoxic agent), maintaining the binding characteristics of the parental antibody. Binding affinities of ABBV-399 to c-Met are comparable to ABT-700, suggesting that conjugation to MMAE did not negatively impact the binding capacity of the antibody. In vitro Efficacy and c-Met Expression Correlation: A correlation between c-Met expression level and sensitivity to ABBV-399 was noted, indicating that cells with higher c-Met expression (threshold >100,000 molecules) are more susceptible to ABBV-399-mediated cytotoxicity. Some tumor cell lines with lower c-Met expression also showed sensitivity to ABBV-399, possibly due to specific biological mechanisms like an autocrine HGF loop. In vivo Efficacy and c-Met Expression: ABBV-399 demonstrated significant tumor growth inhibition in various xenograft models, particularly in those with high c-Met expression. The compound's activity was superior to the parental ABT-700, especially in c-Met overexpressed or MET-amplified tumor cells. Efficacy in Tumors Refractory to ABT-700: ABBV-399 effectively induced tumor regression in models that were refractory to ABT-700, indicating its potential as a treatment option for tumors that have developed resistance to other c-Met targeting therapies. Activity in PDX (Patient-Derived Xenograft) Models: ABBV-399 showed activity in PDX models, suggesting its potential effectiveness in a clinical setting, as PDX models closely mimic human tumor heterogeneity and behavior. The compound's activity varied with c-Met expression levels, being more effective in models with higher c-Met expression. Combination Therapy Potential: ABBV-399 demonstrated enhanced potency when combined with FOLFIRI chemotherapy in colorectal cancer models, indicating potential for combination therapy to improve outcomes and possibly delay or prevent resistance development. The potential for combining ABBV-399 with immunotherapy agents is highlighted, leveraging the possible immune-activating properties of microtubule inhibitor-based ADCs. Safety and Tolerability: Preclinical toxicity studies in cynomolgus monkeys showed that the primary toxicities of ABBV-399 were either nonadverse or reversible. ABBV-399 was well tolerated in a phase I clinical trial at a dose of 2.7 mg/kg, providing a favorable outlook for its safety profile in human therapy. Clinical Implications and Future Directions: The promising preclinical and early clinical results of ABBV-399 support its continued development and assessment as a potential therapeutic option for a broad range of c-Met expressing tumors. The specificity of ABBV-399 to c-Met expressing tumors, coupled with its tolerability and potential for combination therapy, positions it as a potentially significant advancement in targeted cancer therapy.

Literature_Source_Name(s): ROR1 targeting with the antibody-drug conjugate VLS-101 is effective in Richter syndrome patientÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂderived xenograft mouse models
Literature_Source_Comments: Complex Structure of VLS-101: VLS-101 is a sophisticated antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) designed for targeted cancer therapy. It comprises a humanized monoclonal antibody (UC-961), a proteolytically cleavable linker (mc-vc-PAB), and a cytotoxic agent (MMAE). The average drug-to-antibody ratio (DAR) is 4, with a range from 0 to 8, indicating variability in drug loading per antibody molecule. Effective Binding and Internalization: Both UC-961 and VLS-101 demonstrate strong binding to ROR1+ tumor cells, with low effective concentration (IC50) values, indicating their potential effectiveness in targeting tumor cells. Time- and temperature-dependent internalization studies show that both UC-961 and VLS-101 can efficiently enter target cells, which is crucial for delivering the cytotoxic payload inside the cancer cells. Variable ROR1 Expression in Different Models: There is variability in the expression of ROR1 among different RS-PDXÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂderived cell models, with some showing very high and others minimal expression. This variability is crucial for understanding the potential responsiveness of different cancer types to VLS-101 treatment. Despite the variability in expression levels, ROR1 expression remained stable over time in the RS-PDX models. Limited Signaling Capability of ROR1 in RS Cells: While ROR1 is a good target for the ADC, its signaling capability in RS cells is lower compared to CLL cells, indicating that ROR1's role as a signaling molecule in RS is limited. This observation emphasizes the importance of the cytotoxic mechanism of VLS-101 rather than relying on altering signaling pathways for its effectiveness. Effective Tumor Targeting and Cytotoxicity of VLS-101: VLS-101 induces cell-cycle arrest and apoptosis in ROR1+ RS cells, demonstrating its potential as an effective treatment option. The drug shows significant tumor growth inhibition in subcutaneous RS-PDX models, highlighting its potential efficacy in vivo. Specificity and Bystander Effect of VLS-101: The treatment is highly specific to ROR1 expression, as demonstrated by the lack of effect on the ROR1ÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂ RS9737 model. This specificity is crucial for minimizing off-target effects and improving safety. A bystander effect is suggested, where the cytotoxic drug can affect adjacent tumor cells that do not express ROR1, potentially enhancing the therapeutic impact of VLS-101. Potential for Clinical Application and Need for Diagnostic Tools: The findings support the use of ROR1-targeted ADC therapy for treating patients with RS, especially those who have experienced disease transformation. The development and validation of diagnostic tools for determining ROR1 expression in patients are crucial for the effective application of this therapy. Different methods like flow cytometry, qRT-PCR, and IHC have their advantages and limitations, and their suitability needs to be evaluated in clinical settings. Ongoing Clinical Trial: A phase 1 clinical trial of VLS-101 is currently ongoing, emphasizing the translational potential of this research and its relevance for patient care.

Literature_Source_Comments: We developed a unique conjugation technology, which enables site-specific modification of certain glutamine (Gln, Q) amino acids in proteins by an engineered microbial transglutaminase (mTgase). Literature_Source_Name(s): Characterization of SGN-CD123A, A Potent CD123-Directed AntibodyÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂDrug Conjugate for Acute Myeloid Leukemia
Literature_Source_Comments: CD123 Expression in AML Patients: A significant number of Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) patients exhibit CD123 expression on the surface of leukemic blasts, confirmed by multicolor flow cytometry in a study of 52 patients. CD123 expression was noted in 92% of the patients, with a notable increase (10-fold) compared to the background in 94% of these cases. These observations underline the potential of CD123 as a target for antibody-based therapies in AML treatment. Development of SGN-CD123A (Antibody-Drug Conjugate - ADC): Creation of SGN-CD123A involved humanizing a murine anti-CD123 antibody and modifying it to allow site-specific conjugation, followed by linking it with the pyrrolobenzodiazepine (PBD) payload and a valineÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂalanine dipeptide linker. The ADC demonstrates strong binding affinity to CD123 and rapid internalization into AML cells, leading to the delivery of the cytotoxic payload directly to the target cells. In Vitro Cytotoxic Activity of SGN-CD123A: SGN-CD123A showed high cytotoxic activity across various AML cell lines, including multidrug resistance (MDR) cell lines, suggesting its potential to overcome MDR mechanisms. The cytotoxic activity of SGN-CD123A was specific to CD123 expressing cells, indicating targeted action with minimal impact on non-target cells. In Vivo Efficacy of SGN-CD123A: In vivo studies using xenograft models demonstrated significant anti-leukemic activity of SGN-CD123A, leading to extended survival and remission in treated mice. Remarkable reduction in tumor burden and leukemia blast cells was observed in patient-derived xenograft (PDX) models, indicating the potential of SGN-CD123A to target leukemic stem cells. Combination Therapy Potential: Studies indicate a synergy between SGN-CD123A and quizartinib, a FLT3-ITD specific inhibitor, especially in FLT3 mutated AML xenograft models. The combination therapy showed promising results, with significant tumor regression and improved survival, suggesting potential clinical benefits in treating AML, especially with FLT3 mutations. Challenges and Considerations for AML Treatment: Despite advancements, resistance to treatments, especially in refractory or relapsed cases, remains a significant challenge. The presence of leukemic stem cells and resistance to targeted therapies are critical hurdles in achieving long-term remission. Combining targeted therapies like FLT3 inhibitors with CD123-targeted drugs might address these challenges by targeting overlapping pathways and potentially reducing the risk of resistance development. Implications for Future Research and Treatment: The high activity of SGN-CD123A against various AML models, irrespective of their cytogenetic profiles and MDR status, supports the exploration of its clinical utility. Ongoing clinical trials and further research are essential to establish the efficacy, safety, and best combination regimens for SGN-CD123A in AML treatment. Understanding the potential on-target but off-disease side effects due to the expression of CD123 on hematopoietic progenitor cells is crucial for developing safe treatment protocols.

Literature_Source_Name(s): Targeting CD74 in multiple myeloma with the novel, site-specific antibody-drug conjugate STRO-001
Literature_Source_Comments: STRO-001 Development and Properties: SP7219, the anti-CD74 antibody in STRO-001, was discovered from a Fab-ribosome display library and selected for optimal properties in cell binding, internalization, affinity, and stability. STRO-001 was generated using Sutro's XpressCF+ÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂ¢ platform, a cell-free protein synthesis technology. STRO-001 is an antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) designed for targeted cancer therapy, specifically targeting CD74 with a non-cleavable maytansinoid linker-warhead. CD74 Expression in Multiple Myeloma (MM): CD74 expression was analyzed in bone marrow specimens from MM patients, showing high levels in both newly diagnosed and relapsed/refractory patients. mRNA expression analysis from the CoMMpass study indicated high CD74 expression, suggesting its potential as a reliable target for therapeutic intervention. Cytotoxicity and Cell Line Studies: STRO-001 demonstrated potent cell killing activity in MM cell lines, with a notable observation that even cell lines with low CD74 expression were effectively targeted. Two catabolites of STRO-001 were identified, SC246 and SC401, which displayed significantly less cytotoxicity than the parent compound, potentially contributing to a favorable safety profile. In Vivo Efficacy of STRO-001 in MM Models: STRO-001 showed significant anti-tumor activity in disseminated MM models, effectively prolonging survival and reducing tumor burden. The dose-response relationship of STRO-001 was evaluated, showing that even a single dose could significantly suppress tumor growth and prolong survival. STRO-001 Binding and Activity in Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma (NHL): STRO-001 and its antibody component, SP7219, exhibited high affinity binding to CD74 in human and cynomolgus monkey, but not in mouse. STRO-001 showed potent cytotoxicity against various NHL cell lines, indicating its potential for treating different NHL subtypes. CD74 Expression in Human and Cynomolgus Monkey: CD74 expression was predominantly observed in B cells from healthy human and cynomolgus monkey bone marrow aspirates, supporting its role as a target in B-cell malignancies. Differential Effects on NaÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂ¯ve vs. Proliferating B Cells: STRO-001 exhibited more potent cytotoxicity against proliferating B cells compared to naÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂ¯ve B cells, indicating its potential effectiveness against actively dividing cancer cells. In Vivo Anti-Tumor Activity in DLBCL and MCL Models: STRO-001 displayed significant anti-tumor activity in DLBCL and MCL xenograft models, indicating its therapeutic potential against these malignancies. The observed activity was dose-dependent and led to complete tumor regression in certain models. Safety and Pharmacodynamic Profiling: In cynomolgus monkeys, STRO-001 was tolerated at certain dosages but exhibited dose-limiting toxicities at higher levels, primarily affecting hematopoietic/lymphoid tissues. Immunophenotyping analysis revealed dose-dependent decreases in B-cell and monocyte populations, highlighting the importance of dose management to balance efficacy and safety. Clinical Implications and Ongoing Studies: The consistent CD74 expression in patient samples, coupled with the in vitro and in vivo efficacy of STRO-001, supports the clinical investigation of this ADC in MM and B-cell malignancies. A phase 1 clinical study is currently investigating STRO-001 in patients with MM and B-cell malignancies, aiming to further validate its therapeutic potential and safety profile.

Literature_Source_Name(s): ABBV-399, a c-Met AntibodyÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂDrug Conjugate that Targets Both METÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂAmplified and c-MetÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂOverexpressing Tumors, Irrespective of MET Pathway Dependence
Literature_Source_Comments: Conjugation and Binding Properties: ABBV-399 is a conjugate of ABT-700 and vcMMAE (a cytotoxic agent), maintaining the binding characteristics of the parental antibody. Binding affinities of ABBV-399 to c-Met are comparable to ABT-700, suggesting that conjugation to MMAE did not negatively impact the binding capacity of the antibody. In vitro Efficacy and c-Met Expression Correlation: A correlation between c-Met expression level and sensitivity to ABBV-399 was noted, indicating that cells with higher c-Met expression (threshold >100,000 molecules) are more susceptible to ABBV-399-mediated cytotoxicity. Some tumor cell lines with lower c-Met expression also showed sensitivity to ABBV-399, possibly due to specific biological mechanisms like an autocrine HGF loop. In vivo Efficacy and c-Met Expression: ABBV-399 demonstrated significant tumor growth inhibition in various xenograft models, particularly in those with high c-Met expression. The compound's activity was superior to the parental ABT-700, especially in c-Met overexpressed or MET-amplified tumor cells. Efficacy in Tumors Refractory to ABT-700: ABBV-399 effectively induced tumor regression in models that were refractory to ABT-700, indicating its potential as a treatment option for tumors that have developed resistance to other c-Met targeting therapies. Activity in PDX (Patient-Derived Xenograft) Models: ABBV-399 showed activity in PDX models, suggesting its potential effectiveness in a clinical setting, as PDX models closely mimic human tumor heterogeneity and behavior. The compound's activity varied with c-Met expression levels, being more effective in models with higher c-Met expression. Combination Therapy Potential: ABBV-399 demonstrated enhanced potency when combined with FOLFIRI chemotherapy in colorectal cancer models, indicating potential for combination therapy to improve outcomes and possibly delay or prevent resistance development. The potential for combining ABBV-399 with immunotherapy agents is highlighted, leveraging the possible immune-activating properties of microtubule inhibitor-based ADCs. Safety and Tolerability: Preclinical toxicity studies in cynomolgus monkeys showed that the primary toxicities of ABBV-399 were either nonadverse or reversible. ABBV-399 was well tolerated in a phase I clinical trial at a dose of 2.7 mg/kg, providing a favorable outlook for its safety profile in human therapy. Clinical Implications and Future Directions: The promising preclinical and early clinical results of ABBV-399 support its continued development and assessment as a potential therapeutic option for a broad range of c-Met expressing tumors. The specificity of ABBV-399 to c-Met expressing tumors, coupled with its tolerability and potential for combination therapy, positions it as a potentially significant advancement in targeted cancer therapy.

Literature_Source_Name(s): Potent Anticarcinoma Activity of the Humanized Anti-CD70 Antibody h1F6 Conjugated to the Tubulin Inhibitor Auristatin via an Uncleavable Linker
Literature_Source_Comments: Observations from the study: CD70 Expression in RCC Tumors: Intense CD70 expression was confirmed in both primary and metastatic RCC tumor cell lines grown in mice. Primary patient-derived RCC tumors and standard tumor cell lines showed similar levels of CD70 expression, validating them as suitable preclinical models for anti-CD70 antibody-drug conjugate studies. Development and Efficacy of h1F6-mcMMAF Conjugates: h1F6-mcMMAF(4) and h1F6-mcMMAF(8) conjugates were synthesized and tested, showing binding to CD70+ target cells. h1F6-mcMMAF(4) demonstrated dose-dependent cytotoxicity in RCC cell lines and inhibited the proliferation of primary RCC cultures. Antitumor Effects in RCC Xenograft Models: Treatment with h1F6-mcMMAF(4) significantly inhibited tumor growth and reduced tumor size in various RCC xenograft models. The study established a target dose range for h1F6-mcMMAF(4) between 0.5 and 4.5 mg/kg for lead identification studies. Comparison of Different Drug Loading Ratios: h1F6-mcMMAF(8) showed higher in vitro potency compared to h1F6-mcMMAF(4). However, in vivo antitumor activities of h1F6-mcMMAF(4) and h1F6-mcMMAF(8) in RCC and glioblastoma models were comparable, questioning the direct correlation between drug loading and therapeutic efficacy in vivo. Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics: Both h1F6-mcMMAF(4) and h1F6-mcMMAF(8) exhibited similar pharmacokinetic characteristics with comparable serum half-lives. In vivo studies indicate that the drug-linker combinations for different therapeutic targets should be identified based on in vivo data rather than in vitro studies alone. Predictive Value of RCC Tumor Models: Orthotopic RCC models using primary patient RCC tumor cell isolates showed moderate to high CD70 expression and predicted the efficacy of compounds in clinical settings with high accuracy. These models are crucial for generating preclinical efficacy data predictive of clinical outcomes. Therapeutic Potential and Development Considerations: h1F6-mcMMAF conjugates, particularly the SGN-75 (h1F6-mcMMAF(4)), demonstrated potent antitumor responses and complete tumor regressions at low dose levels. Given the equipotency between 4- and 8-loaded compounds and the reduced off-target toxicity potential, h1F6-mcMMAF(4) emerges as a promising candidate for clinical development in solid tumor indications.

Literature_Source_Name(s): Design of Coltuximab Ravtansine, a CD19-Targeting AntibodyÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂDrug Conjugate (ADC) for the Treatment of B-Cell Malignancies: StructureÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂActivity Relationships and Preclinical Evaluation
Characterization of SGN-CD123A, A Potent CD123-Directed AntibodyÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂDrug Conjugate for Acute Myeloid Leukemia
Literature_Source_Comments: CD123 Expression in AML Patients: A significant number of Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) patients exhibit CD123 expression on the surface of leukemic blasts, confirmed by multicolor flow cytometry in a study of 52 patients. CD123 expression was noted in 92% of the patients, with a notable increase (10-fold) compared to the background in 94% of these cases. These observations underline the potential of CD123 as a target for antibody-based therapies in AML treatment. Development of SGN-CD123A (Antibody-Drug Conjugate - ADC): Creation of SGN-CD123A involved humanizing a murine anti-CD123 antibody and modifying it to allow site-specific conjugation, followed by linking it with the pyrrolobenzodiazepine (PBD) payload and a valineÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂalanine dipeptide linker. The ADC demonstrates strong binding affinity to CD123 and rapid internalization into AML cells, leading to the delivery of the cytotoxic payload directly to the target cells. In Vitro Cytotoxic Activity of SGN-CD123A: SGN-CD123A showed high cytotoxic activity across various AML cell lines, including multidrug resistance (MDR) cell lines, suggesting its potential to overcome MDR mechanisms. The cytotoxic activity of SGN-CD123A was specific to CD123 expressing cells, indicating targeted action with minimal impact on non-target cells. In Vivo Efficacy of SGN-CD123A: In vivo studies using xenograft models demonstrated significant anti-leukemic activity of SGN-CD123A, leading to extended survival and remission in treated mice. Remarkable reduction in tumor burden and leukemia blast cells was observed in patient-derived xenograft (PDX) models, indicating the potential of SGN-CD123A to target leukemic stem cells. Combination Therapy Potential: Studies indicate a synergy between SGN-CD123A and quizartinib, a FLT3-ITD specific inhibitor, especially in FLT3 mutated AML xenograft models. The combination therapy showed promising results, with significant tumor regression and improved survival, suggesting potential clinical benefits in treating AML, especially with FLT3 mutations. Challenges and Considerations for AML Treatment: Despite advancements, resistance to treatments, especially in refractory or relapsed cases, remains a significant challenge. The presence of leukemic stem cells and resistance to targeted therapies are critical hurdles in achieving long-term remission. Combining targeted therapies like FLT3 inhibitors with CD123-targeted drugs might address these challenges by targeting overlapping pathways and potentially reducing the risk of resistance development. Implications for Future Research and Treatment: The high activity of SGN-CD123A against various AML models, irrespective of their cytogenetic profiles and MDR status, supports the exploration of its clinical utility. Ongoing clinical trials and further research are essential to establish the efficacy, safety, and best combination regimens for SGN-CD123A in AML treatment. Understanding the potential on-target but off-disease side effects due to the expression of CD123 on hematopoietic progenitor cells is crucial for developing safe treatment protocols.

RN765C, a low affinity EGFR antibody drug conjugate with potent anti-tumor activity in preclinical solid tumor models
Literature_Source_Comments: A broad distribution of DAR can negatively impact both efficacy and safety profiles: low DAR species reduce the potency of ADC while high DAR species are prone to aggregation, increased clearance rate and premature release of the toxic payload during circulation leading to increase off-target toxicity [27]. To circumvent this problem, we and others have developed various site-specific conjugation technologies to generate a more homogeneous ADC with increased stability in the circulation [9, 27-29]. RN765C uses transglutaminase-mediated site-specific conjugation to enable production of a nearly homogeneous EGFR ADCs carrying two payloads (DAR 2) [9, 30]. By making a homogeneous ADC with a DAR of 2, eliminating high DAR species, and utilizing stable isopeptide linkage and incorporating an AcLys-VC-PABC linker that was shown to be more stable in circulation [11, 30, 31], we aimed to reduce both off-target toxicity due to unintended payload release in circulation as well as on-target toxicity due to delivery of high DAR species to EGFR expressing normal tissue. Furthermore, we selected a low affinity EGFR antibody as the backbone for RN765C to achieve differential binding on normal cells versus tumor cells expressing medium to high levels of EGFR. The low affinity nature of RN765C (KD = 338 nM at 37ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂ°C) reduces its toxicity on low EGFR expressing cells such as normal human keratinocytes, which have ÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂ¼ 38,000 EGFR/cell (Table ÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂ(Table2).2). For human keratinocytes it requires ÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂ¼ 70 nM of RN765C to achieve 50% of cell killing in vitro (which is partly driven by non-receptor mediated pinocytosis) whereas the same level of killing can be achieved with sub-nanomolar to low nanomolar of RN765C in many tumor cell lines expressing > 50,000 EGFR/cell (Figure ÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂ(Figure3A,3A, ÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂ,3B3B and ÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂand3C,3C, and Table ÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂTable2).2). Aside from the potential to differentially target tumor cells, the low affinity and fast dissociation rate constant (kd = 0.21 s-1) nature of RN765C may also facilitate tumor penetration as compared with high affinity ADCs
